attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Check scripts use mocked Git runner pattern for testing</title>
  <subtitle>Test infrastructure validates Git operations by mocking command execution and tracking calls</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>check-shared-sync.mjs tests shared knowledge sync functionality using mocked Git commands</fact>
    <fact>Test framework uses assert() helper tracking pass/fail counts with descriptive names</fact>
    <fact>makeRunner() factory creates mock Git executor that intercepts commands like status, add, commit, pull, rev-parse</fact>
    <fact>Mock runner validates working directory (expects /shared), simulates command outputs, and tracks call sequences</fact>
    <fact>Script tests Git workflow scenarios including clean/dirty status, user config, and rebase operations</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The check-shared-sync.mjs script demonstrates the project's testing pattern for Git automation. It uses a mock runner factory that intercepts Git commands and returns simulated outputs, allowing validation of Git workflow logic without actual repository operations. The makeRunner() function accepts configuration for simulating different states (status output, push failures, pulled files) and tracks all Git command calls. The assert() helper provides clear pass/fail reporting. This pattern enables testing complex Git scenarios like sync conflicts, authentication setup, and rebase operations in isolation. This infrastructure shows the project already has sophisticated Git automation testing, which could inform approaches to automating upstream update workflows.</narrative>

\\\"fetch 失敗(看上方錯誤)。\\\");\\n\\n// ── 2. 落後幾個 commit ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\\nconst behind = Number(\\n  git([\\\"rev-list\\\", \\\"--count\\\", \\\"0\\\",\\n);\\nconsole.log(`  ${BASE_BRANCH} 落後 upstream ${behind} 個 commit、領先 ${ahead} 個。`);\\n\\nif (behind === 0) {\\n  console.log(\\\"\\\\n✓ 已是最新,無需同步。\\\");\\n  process.exit(0);\\n}\\n\\n// ── 3. 開拋棄式分支 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\\nstep(`從 ${BASE_BRANCH} 建立 ${ATTEMPT_BRANCH}`);\\nconst sw = git([\\\"switch\\\", \\\"-C\\\", ATTEMPT_BRANCH, BASE_BRANCH]);\\nif (sw.code !== 0) die(1, `建立 ${ATTEMPT_BRANCH} 失敗:${sw.stderr}`);\\n\\n// ── 4. 嘗試 merge ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\\nstep(`merge content.thinking:
This is a significant script creation. Let me break down what was actually built:

1. **What was created**: A new automation script `sync-upstream.mjs` that semi-automates the upstream Git synchronization workflow
2. **What it does**: 
   - Fetches upstream changes
   - Creates a disposable branch for merge attempts
   - Attempts merge and handles conflicts
   - Runs build validation
   - Provides safe rollback options
3. **Safety guarantees**:
   - Never touches main branch directly
   - Never auto-pushes
   - All work done on disposable `sync-attempt` branch
   - Clear exit codes for different scenarios
4. **Exit codes**: 0 (success/up-to-date), 3 (preconditions), 10 (conflicts), 11 (build failure), 1 (other error)

This is a **feature** - new capability added to the project. The script implements a safe, semi-automated workflow for the exact problem the user asked about earlier.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>feature</type>
  <title>Upstream sync automation script created</title>
  <subtitle>Semi-automated script safely merges upstream updates on disposable branch with validation</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>scripts/sync-upstream.mjs automates fetching and merging upstream/main into local fork</fact>
    <fact>Script performs all merge operations on disposable sync-attempt branch, never touching main directly</fact>
    <fact>Pre-flight checks validate clean working directory, no in-progress merges, and upstream remote exists</fact>
    <fact>Automatically runs npm build after successful merge to validate compilation</fact>
    <fact>Exit codes differentiate scenarios: 0 success/up-to-date, 3 preconditions failed, 10 merge conflicts, 11 build failure, 1 other error</fact>
    <fact>Script lists conflicted files and provides remediation guidance for conflict and build failure scenarios</fact>
    <fact>Never auto-pushes; requires manual confirmation to merge sync-attempt back to main and push</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Created sync-upstream.mjs script to automate the upstream synchronization workflow previously done manually. The script follows a safe six-step process: (1) validates preconditions (clean working tree, no pending merges, upstream remote configured), (2) fetches from upstream, (3) checks if behind (exits early if up-to-date), (4) creates disposable sync-attempt branch from main and attempts merge, (5) runs npm build if merge succeeds, (6) provides publishing instructions if all passes. Safety is paramount: main branch is never modified directly, no automatic pushes occur, and clear rollback instructions are provided at each failure point. The script detects merge conflicts and build failures with distinct exit codes, making it suitable for both interactive use and potential CI integration. This addresses the user's original request to automate the upstream update process while maintaining manual approval gates before publishing.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
  </concepts>

    <file>scripts/sync-upstream.mjs</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

The .githooks/pre-commit script runs sync-skills-to-repo.mjs to sync tracked default-skills from local sources, then unconditionally stages the entire default-skills/ directory. This is intentional design to prevent skill definitions from drifting out of sync with their sources.</narrative>

The previous URL configuration likely triggered credential prompts that would block indefinitely in automated contexts or timeout in script execution. The test fetch completed successfully within 30 seconds and revealed upstream has already advanced beyond the sync point (7798a21) established earlier in the session - commits 119a269..fb40782 are now available. This URL fix is critical for the sync-upstream.mjs script to function reliably in future automated sync operations, as the script's fetch step would otherwise hang waiting for interactive credential input that never arrives in automated contexts.</narrative>

This is a significant observation - the sync-upstream.mjs script was run for real against actual upstream divergence. Let me capture the key details:

1. The script successfully detected the repository was 1 commit behind upstream and 6 commits ahead
2. It created the sync-attempt branch and attempted the merge
3. The merge succeeded automatically (no conflicts) - merged upstream/main with changes:
   - Deleted SPEC.md
   - Added docs/SPEC-multi-model-trio.md (206 lines)
   - Added 5 new TypeScript source files: routing-coordinator.ts, routing-log.ts, self-fusion.ts, trio-llm.ts, verifier.ts
   - Modified src/index.ts, src/run-prompt.ts, src/specialist.ts
   - Modified .gitignore
   - Total: 11 files changed, 822 insertions, 111 deletions
4. The build failed (exit code 11 as designed)
5. The script properly stopped at the build failure stage, leaving the user on sync-attempt branch with clear remediation instructions

This demonstrates the script working exactly as designed - it safely attempted the merge on a disposable branch, caught the build failure, and provided clear guidance without corrupting main or auto-pushing.

The upstream changes appear to be a major feature addition around multi-model trio functionality with routing, verification, and self-fusion capabilities.

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Build actually succeeds when run directly after merge</title>
  <subtitle>npm run build exits 0 on sync-attempt branch despite script reporting failure</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>npm run build executes successfully with exit code 0 when run manually on sync-attempt branch</fact>
    <fact>Build script runs tsc -p . which compiles TypeScript without errors</fact>
    <fact>Sync script reported build failure (exit code 11) during automated execution but manual retry succeeds</fact>
    <fact>Possible transient failure during initial build or script exit code detection issue</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Discovered the build failure that halted the sync script was either transient or a false positive. Running npm run build manually on the same sync-attempt branch after the merge completes successfully with exit code 0, executing the simple tsc -p . compilation command without errors. This contradicts the script's exit code 11 (build failure) from moments earlier. Several explanations are possible: (1) a transient issue during the first build attempt that resolved itself (filesystem lag, lock contention, npm cache), (2) the script's exit code detection logic misread the build result, or (3) environmental differences between the script's spawnSync execution context and the current shell. Regardless, the merge from upstream (fb40782) bringing the multi-model trio architecture appears to be clean and compilable. The script's conservative halt-on-error behavior proved useful here - better to stop and verify than to auto-publish potentially broken code, even if this turned out to be a false alarm.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

content.thinking:
The user cleaned up after the sync script test. They:
1. Switched back to main branch
2. Deleted the sync-attempt branch (which was at commit 59748b8 - this contained the merged upstream changes)
3. Popped the stash to restore docs/usage-guide.html
4. Confirmed they're back on main with only the usage guide modified

This is cleanup after testing the sync-upstream.mjs script. The test validated the script works correctly - it detected upstream changes, merged them, and attempted to build (though there was some confusion about whether the build actually failed or not).

The key point is they've abandoned the upstream merge for now (deleted sync-attempt branch) and returned to the state before testing, with just the documentation changes uncommitted.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>change</type>
  <title>Cleaned up test branch and returned to pre-sync state</title>
  <subtitle>Deleted sync-attempt branch and restored working tree to test baseline</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Switched from sync-attempt back to main branch</fact>
    <fact>Deleted sync-attempt branch which was at commit 59748b8 (contained merged upstream changes)</fact>
    <fact>Restored docs/usage-guide.html from stash fe47ec9 back to working tree</fact>
    <fact>Repository now back to pre-test state: main branch at ad00ba8 with only usage guide modified</fact>
    <fact>Upstream merge bringing multi-model trio architecture abandoned for now</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Performed cleanup after successfully testing the sync-upstream.mjs automation script. The test validated the script's core workflow: it detected the repository was 1 commit behind upstream, created the disposable sync-attempt branch, merged cleanly (bringing 11 files including new routing-coordinator, trio-llm, and verifier modules), and attempted to build. Although the build exit code detection reported failure, manual retry showed the merge actually compiled successfully - likely a transient issue or script detection glitch. Regardless, the test accomplished its goal of validating the automation works end-to-end. By deleting the sync-attempt branch, the merged upstream changes (multi-model trio architecture) are discarded for now, allowing the user to decide when to actually pull those updates. The repository returns to its stable state: main at commit ad00ba8 with only the docs/usage-guide.html documentation pending commit. This demonstrates the script's safety design working as intended - all risky operations isolated on throwaway branch, easy cleanup, main never touched.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

content.thinking:
The user is updating the sync-upstream.mjs script documentation to reflect a change in the build validation approach. The change is updating the comments to document that the script will:
1. Run `npm install` after merge (in case dependencies changed)
2. Use `tsc --noEmit` for type checking instead of `npm run build`

The rationale provided is important: `npm run build` emits files to dist/ which can be locked by a running bridge process on Windows, causing false failures. Type checking with `--noEmit` doesn't write files and doesn't compete for locks, while still validating that the merged code compiles.

This is updating documentation/comments only so far - the actual implementation change hasn't happened yet in this edit.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>change</type>
  <title>Updated sync script documentation for Windows-safe validation</title>
  <subtitle>Changed comments to document tsc --noEmit approach avoiding file lock conflicts</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Modified scripts/sync-upstream.mjs documentation header to describe new validation approach</fact>
    <fact>Script will use tsc --noEmit for type checking instead of npm run build to avoid Windows file locking issues</fact>
    <fact>Rationale added: npm run build emits to dist/ which gets locked by running bridge process on Windows causing false failures</fact>
    <fact>Type checking with --noEmit validates compilation without writing files or competing for locks</fact>
    <fact>Exit code description updated from "build 失敗" to "編譯失敗" (build failed → compilation failed)</fact>
    <fact>Documentation now mentions npm install step to handle dependency changes from upstream</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Updated the sync-upstream.mjs script's documentation to reflect a more robust validation strategy that avoids Windows-specific issues. The previous approach using npm run build encountered false failures when the Telegram bridge process was running, because build output to dist/ would be blocked by file locks on the running JavaScript modules. The documented solution switches to tsc --noEmit for type checking, which validates the merged code compiles correctly without emitting any files, eliminating lock contention. The documentation also now includes an npm install step after merge to ensure dependency changes from upstream are properly installed before validation. This change addresses the likely root cause of the exit code 11 "failure" observed during testing - the build may have failed due to file locks rather than actual compilation errors. The rationale is clearly documented in comments explaining the Windows-specific file locking behavior and why type-only validation is sufficient for the sync script's safety checks.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
    <concept>why-it-exists</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>scripts/sync-upstream.mjs</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

The user is making iterative improvements to the sync-upstream.mjs script based on the test experience. Let me track these changes:

1. First edit: Updated documentation to describe using tsc --noEmit instead of npm run build, added rationale about Windows file locking
2. Second edit: Replaced the single "npm run build" step with two steps:
   - Step 5a: npm install to sync dependencies that may have changed from upstream
   - Step 5b: npx tsc --noEmit for type checking without emitting files to avoid lock conflicts
3. Third edit: Updated success message from "build 通過" to "型別檢查通過" for consistency

These changes implement the solution to the Windows file locking problem discovered during testing.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>feature</type>
  <title>Implemented Windows-safe validation with dependency sync</title>
  <subtitle>Replaced build step with npm install plus tsc --noEmit to avoid file lock conflicts</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Replaced single npm run build step with two-phase validation: npm install then npx tsc --noEmit</fact>
    <fact>Step 5a runs npm install to sync dependencies that may have changed in upstream merge</fact>
    <fact>Step 5b runs tsc --noEmit for type checking without emitting dist/ files</fact>
    <fact>Type-only validation avoids file lock conflicts with running bridge process on Windows</fact>
    <fact>Success message updated from "build 通過" to "型別檢查通過" for accuracy</fact>
    <fact>Both npm and npx commands use platform-specific .cmd extensions on Windows</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Implemented the Windows-safe validation strategy documented in the earlier comment updates. The script now performs two distinct validation steps after successful merge: first npm install ensures dependencies from upstream are synchronized (addressing the case where upstream adds or updates packages), then npx tsc --noEmit performs pure type checking without emitting compiled output. This avoids the file locking issue discovered during testing where npm run build would fail when the bridge process was running because it couldn't write to locked dist/*.js files. The --noEmit approach validates the merge is compilable without competing for file locks, since type checking doesn't write any output. Actual dist/ compilation happens when start.bat launches the bridge. Both steps properly detect Windows platform and use .cmd extensions for npm/npx commands. Error messages differentiate between npm install failures (dependency issues) and type check failures (compilation errors), each providing appropriate remediation guidance while preserving the safe rollback path.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>scripts/sync-upstream.mjs</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

  <type>refactor</type>
  <title>Amended automation commit with Windows-safe validation improvements</title>
  <subtitle>Updated commit 6b64c73 with npm install and tsc --noEmit changes from testing feedback</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Amended commit ad00ba8 with sync-upstream.mjs improvements, creating new commit 6b64c73</fact>
    <fact>Total changes now 269 insertions and 12 deletions, up from 251 insertions (18 additional lines)</fact>
    <fact>Commit bundles original automation script plus Windows file lock fix and dependency sync</fact>
    <fact>Working tree now clean after staging script modifications</fact>
    <fact>Commit message unchanged, still describes safe halt-on-conflict helper design</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Amended the automation script commit to incorporate the Windows-safe validation improvements discovered during testing. The commit SHA changed from ad00ba8 to 6b64c73 as expected with git commit --amend. The additional 18 lines (269 vs 251 insertions) reflect the split of step 5 into npm install (dependency sync) and tsc --noEmit (type checking), plus expanded documentation explaining why the script avoids npm run build to prevent Windows file lock conflicts with running bridge processes. The commit now represents the complete, tested automation solution: the original safe merge workflow plus the platform-specific robustness improvements. The working tree is clean and ready for the next test iteration. This demonstrates good development practice: iterate based on testing feedback, amend to keep related changes together, and maintain clean commit boundaries between concerns.</narrative>

  </concepts>

content.thinking:
The sync script ran and successfully completed steps 0-4:
- Precondition checks passed
- Fetched upstream 
- Detected 1 commit behind, 6 ahead
- Created sync-attempt branch
- Merged upstream/main cleanly (brought multi-model trio architecture)

But it failed at step 5a (npm install) with exit code 11. The script is working correctly - it detected a failure during dependency installation and halted safely on the sync-attempt branch without touching main.

This is interesting - npm install failed after the merge. This could indicate:
1. Dependency conflicts
2. Network issues
3. Package.json changes from upstream causing incompatibilities
4. npm cache issues

The script is behaving correctly: it attempted to sync dependencies (which the upstream merge may have changed), detected the failure, and halted with clear remediation instructions. Main branch remains untouched.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Script halted on npm install failure after clean merge</title>
  <subtitle>Upstream merge succeeded but dependency installation failed, script exited code 11 safely</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Script successfully completed all precondition checks and upstream fetch</fact>
    <fact>Created sync-attempt branch and merged upstream/main cleanly without conflicts</fact>
    <fact>Merge brought multi-model trio architecture: 11 files changed, 822 insertions, 111 deletions</fact>
    <fact>npm install step failed after successful merge, causing exit code 11</fact>
    <fact>Script halted safely on sync-attempt branch with main untouched and clear rollback guidance</fact>
    <fact>New validation workflow caught dependency issues before type checking step</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Second test run of the improved sync script demonstrates the two-phase validation working as designed. The script executed flawlessly through the merge: validated preconditions, fetched upstream, detected repository divergence (1 behind, 6 ahead), created the disposable sync-attempt branch, and merged upstream/main cleanly. The merge successfully brought the multi-model trio architecture with routing coordinator, verifier, self-fusion, and trio-llm modules. However, the newly added npm install step detected a dependency installation failure before attempting type checking. This is exactly the scenario the script is designed to handle: catch problems early, halt safely on the throwaway branch, leave main pristine, and provide clear remediation instructions. The failure could indicate package.json changes from upstream created dependency conflicts, network issues, or npm configuration problems. The script's defensive two-phase approach (install dependencies → type check) proved valuable - it found issues that would have been hidden if validation jumped straight to compilation using existing node_modules.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
  </concepts>

  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

content.thinking:
This is interesting - npm install actually succeeded with exit code 0 when run manually, but the script reported it as a failure. This is similar to the earlier build failure that turned out to be a false positive. Let me document this finding.

The output shows:
- npm install completed successfully (exit code 0)
- All dependencies are up to date
- MCP configurations were already set up
- Skills were already present
- 286 packages audited with 12 vulnerabilities (not causing failure)

So the script's npm install step falsely detected a failure. This could be:
1. Exit code detection issue in the script's spawnSync handling
2. stderr output being interpreted as failure
3. Timing or buffering issue

This is a gotcha that needs to be addressed in the automation script.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>npm install actually succeeds despite script reporting failure</title>
  <subtitle>Manual npm install exits 0 on sync-attempt branch, revealing false positive detection</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Running npm install manually on sync-attempt branch completes successfully with exit code 0</fact>
    <fact>npm install output shows "up to date, audited 286 packages" and "All up to date" for dependencies</fact>
    <fact>MCP configurations (kiro, codex, claude) and default skills already present and configured</fact>
    <fact>12 npm audit vulnerabilities present but don't cause installation failure</fact>
    <fact>Script incorrectly detected npm install failure and exited code 11</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Discovered the script's npm install failure was a false positive. Running npm install directly on the same sync-attempt branch succeeds immediately with exit code 0, showing all 286 packages up to date and MCP/skills configurations already in place. This mirrors the earlier false build failure where the script reported exit code 11 but manual retry succeeded. The pattern suggests the script's spawnSync exit code detection may be unreliable - possibly misinterpreting stderr output (like npm audit warnings) as failure, or experiencing timing issues with how Node.js captures process exit codes. The npm audit warnings about 12 vulnerabilities (1 low, 3 moderate, 7 high, 1 critical) are informational and don't cause npm install to fail. This false-positive behavior significantly undermines the automation's reliability - it's stopping on phantom failures rather than real problems. The script needs refined exit code handling to distinguish actual failures from successful operations that produce stderr warnings.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
  </concepts>

  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

content.thinking:
The user is cleaning up after testing the sync script again. They're switching back to main from sync-attempt and deleting the test branch. This is the same cleanup pattern as before - they're abandoning the test merge of the multi-model trio architecture and returning to the baseline state.

The sync-attempt branch was at commit 69a32fb (which would have contained the merged upstream changes). By deleting it, they're discarding that merge test again.

This is a straightforward cleanup operation after discovering the npm install false positive issue.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>change</type>
  <title>Cleaned up test branch after discovering false positive failures</title>
  <subtitle>Returned to main and deleted sync-attempt containing upstream merge test</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Switched from sync-attempt branch back to main branch</fact>
    <fact>Deleted sync-attempt branch at commit 69a32fb containing merged multi-model trio architecture</fact>
    <fact>Second round of testing revealed npm install false positive similar to earlier build failure</fact>
    <fact>Repository back to baseline: main at 6b64c73 with sync script ready but untested successfully end-to-end</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Performed cleanup after the second test iteration revealed persistent false positive exit code detection in the sync script. The sync-attempt branch successfully merged upstream's multi-model trio changes and npm install actually completed without errors, but the script's spawnSync exit code handling incorrectly reported failure and halted with exit code 11. By switching back to main and deleting the test branch, the repository returns to its stable state. This second false positive (first with npm run build, now with npm install) points to a systematic issue with how the script detects command success/failure - likely misinterpreting stderr output as errors even when the process exits 0. The automation script exists and documents the correct approach, but needs debugging of its exit code detection logic before it can reliably automate upstream syncs without false alarms.</narrative>

[\\\"ignore\\\", \\\"pipe\\\", \\\"pipe\\\"] : \\\"inherit\\\",\\n  });\\n  return {\\n    code: res.status ?? 1,\\n    stdout: (res.stdout ?? \\\"\\\").trim(),\\n    stderr: (res.stderr ?? \\\"\\\").trim(),\\n  };\\n}\\n\\nfunction die(code, msg) {\\n  console.error(`\\\\n✗ ${msg}`);\\n  process.exit(code);\\n}\\n\\nfunction step(msg) {\\n  console.log(`\\\\n▸ ${msg}`);\\n}\\n\\n// ── 0. 前置檢查 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\\nstep(\\\"檢查前置條件\\\");\\n\\nif (git([\\\"rev-parse\\\", \\\"--git-dir\\\"]).code !== 0) {\\n  die(3, \\\"不在 git repo content.thinking:
This is an important update to the documentation. The user is revising the approach based on the testing failures. The key changes:

1. Removing npm install from the automated flow
2. Adding rationale for why:
   - Dependencies rarely change between syncs
   - npm install triggers postinstall hooks (MCP/skill configuration with side effects)
   - If new dependencies are added, tsc will report "Cannot find module" errors clearly
   - User can manually npm install at that point
3. Noting that direct node execution will be used instead of npx.cmd to avoid Windows .cmd shim exit code false positives

This is documenting the design tradeoffs and simplifying the validation to just type checking, removing the problematic npm install step that was causing false failures.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>change</type>
  <title>Revised sync script to skip npm install due to false positives</title>
  <subtitle>Updated documentation to remove npm install step and explain design tradeoffs</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Removed npm install from step 5 validation workflow based on testing failures</fact>
    <fact>Added "設計取捨" (design tradeoffs) section documenting three key decisions</fact>
    <fact>Rationale 1: Use tsc --noEmit instead of npm run build to avoid Windows file lock conflicts</fact>
    <fact>Rationale 2: Use direct node execution instead of npx.cmd to avoid Windows .cmd shim exit code false positives</fact>
    <fact>Rationale 3: Skip npm install because dependencies rarely change and project's postinstall has side effects (MCP/skill configuration)</fact>
    <fact>If upstream adds new dependencies, tsc will report "Cannot find module" errors clearly for manual intervention</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Revised the sync script's documentation to reflect lessons learned from testing the false positive exit code detection issues. The two-phase npm install + tsc approach is being simplified to just type checking, with detailed rationale documented in a new "設計取捨" section. The key insight: npm install rarely needs to run for upstream syncs (dependencies don't change often), and this project's npm install triggers postinstall hooks that configure MCP servers and skills - side effects inappropriate for an automated sync validation step. By skipping npm install, the script avoids both the false positive exit code detection issues observed during testing and unwanted side effects. If upstream does add new dependencies, TypeScript compilation will fail with clear "Cannot find module" errors, prompting manual npm install. The documentation also notes the plan to use direct node execution instead of npx.cmd to sidestep Windows command shim exit code reliability issues. This represents a pragmatic simplification: validate only what's necessary (type correctness), defer dependency installation to manual intervention when actually needed.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>why-it-exists</concept>
    <concept>trade-off</concept>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>scripts/sync-upstream.mjs</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

content.thinking:
This is a significant implementation change. The user is removing the npm install step completely and changing how tsc is invoked. Let me understand what's happening:

1. Removed the npm install step entirely (previously step 5a)
2. Changed from running `npx tsc` or `npx.cmd tsc` to running `node node_modules/typescript/bin/tsc`
3. Uses `process.execPath` to get the node binary path directly
4. Updated error message to specifically mention "Cannot find module" case suggesting manual npm install

This implements the design decisions documented in the previous comment edit:
- Skip npm install to avoid false positives from .cmd shims and unwanted postinstall side effects
- Use direct node execution instead of npx to get reliable exit codes
- If dependencies are missing, tsc will fail with clear "Cannot find module" errors

This is a pragmatic solution to the false positive issues encountered during testing.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>feature</type>
  <title>Implemented direct TypeScript execution avoiding npm/npx wrappers</title>
  <subtitle>Removed npm install step and switched to node-direct tsc execution for reliable exit codes</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Removed npm install step 5a completely from validation workflow</fact>
    <fact>Changed TypeScript execution from npx/npx.cmd wrapper to direct node node_modules/typescript/bin/tsc invocation</fact>
    <fact>Uses process.execPath to run tsc directly, avoiding Windows .cmd shim exit code issues</fact>
    <fact>Updated error message to provide specific guidance: if "Cannot find module" error occurs, run npm install manually</fact>
    <fact>Simplified from two-phase validation (install → typecheck) to single-phase type checking only</fact>
    <fact>Comment header updated to "node 直跑 tsc --noEmit;不 emit、不搶 dist/ 鎖、exit code 可靠" documenting reliability focus</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Implemented the final design after testing revealed persistent false positive exit code detection with npm/npx commands on Windows. The script now executes TypeScript compilation directly using process.execPath (the node binary) with an explicit path to node_modules/typescript/bin/tsc, completely bypassing npx and its .cmd shim layer that was causing unreliable exit code reporting. The npm install step is removed entirely based on the rationale that dependencies rarely change between upstream syncs, and this project's npm install triggers postinstall hooks with side effects (MCP server and skill configuration). If upstream does introduce new dependencies, TypeScript will fail with clear "Cannot find module" errors, and the updated error message specifically instructs users to run npm install manually before retrying. This simplification trades automatic dependency installation for reliability: the script now validates only type correctness using a known-reliable execution path, avoiding the false positives that plagued earlier iterations. The comment emphasizes three guarantees: no file emission, no dist/ lock contention, and reliable exit codes.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>trade-off</concept>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>scripts/sync-upstream.mjs</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

The final implementation using direct node execution (process.execPath with explicit path to tsc binary) eliminated the false positive exit code detection that plagued earlier versions using npm run build and npx. The script flawlessly executed its six-step workflow: validated preconditions (clean working tree, no pending merges), fetched from upstream, detected divergence (1 behind, 6 ahead), created disposable sync-attempt branch, merged upstream/main cleanly, and validated TypeScript compilation. The merge successfully brought the multi-model trio architecture with routing coordinator, verifier, self-fusion, and trio-llm modules. Type checking passed, confirming the merge is compilable. The script exited with code 0 and provided clear next steps for publishing: switch to main, fast-forward merge sync-attempt, push to origin, delete temporary branch. This validates the complete automation workflow the user originally requested - a reliable way to sync upstream updates without manual Git commands.</narrative>
